planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in fall or softwood cuttings in summer; plant container-grown shrubs in almost any well-drained soil in full sun to part shade. Forsythia blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering to preserve next year's buds. Hard pruning in late summer or fall removes next spring's flower buds.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; forsythia is the most widely planted early-spring flowering shrub in the temperate world — a hybrid of two Asian species producing cascading arches of branches covered, before any leaves appear, with bright, clear-yellow flowers in late February through April, depending on location. The intensity of color — pure, saturated spring-yellow on bare branches against the still-dormant landscape — makes it one of the most visually arresting events of the early garden year. After the flowers fade, the arching branches clothe themselves in dark-green foliage that provides a pleasant, medium-textured hedge or background through summer. It is among the most cold-hardy, drought-tolerant, and adaptable flowering shrubs available — growing in clay, sand, poor soil, and partial shade without complaint. The cultivar 'Lynwood Gold' (also sold as 'Lynwood Variety') is the most widely planted, with an upright habit and consistently large, fully open flowers. 'Meadowlark' is exceptionally cold-hardy to zone 4. Pruning should be done only immediately after flowering.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown shrubs in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; pearl bush blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering. The cultivar 'The Bride' is compact, heavily flowering, and significantly better than the straight species, and is the most widely available selection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; pearl bush is an underappreciated Asian flowering shrub producing, in mid-spring, an extraordinary display of pure-white flowers in rounded clusters that cover the plant so densely the individual flowers resemble a cascade of lustrous white pearls — a display that rivals the visual impact of the best spring flowering shrubs. The flowers are 5-petaled, circular, and exquisitely symmetrical, giving the plant its common name. Unlike many early-spring shrubs, pearl bush has a refined and elegant quality rather than blowsy exuberance. The cultivar 'The Bride' is a compact, densely flowering form that spreads wider than tall (3–4 feet tall, 4–6 feet wide) and has become the preferred selection for small modern gardens. It is long-lived and low-maintenance, requiring only light pruning immediately after flowering and an appropriate site that does not become waterlogged. An excellent alternative to spireas and deutzias for the spring border.

planting.note: Sow seeds or divide established clumps in early spring in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sheep's fescue is intolerant of heavy, wet, clay soils and humid summer heat — it performs best in cool, dry climates and partial shade in hot summer regions. Cut or comb out dead foliage in early spring. Note: F. glauca (blue fescue) is a closely related and often confused species with more consistently blue-gray foliage.

directions: Sow in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sheep's fescue is a fine-textured, drought-tolerant cool-season bunchgrass native to poor, dry, thin soils of European mountains and moorlands, widely used as an ornamental edging grass, meadow component, and low-maintenance lawn grass in cool, dry climates. The narrow, wiry, blue-green to gray-blue leaves form tight, rounded tufts that remain attractive through most of the year — evergreen in mild winters, dormant in summer heat. The related Festuca glauca (blue fescue), with more intensely blue-gray coloring in selected cultivars like 'Elijah Blue,' is the standard ornamental garden selection. Sheep's fescue itself is used more in low-maintenance lawn seed mixes for poor, dry sites including sandy banks and dry slopes where conventional lawn grasses fail. Among the most drought-tolerant of all cool-season turf species, requiring little supplemental water once established in appropriate conditions.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; the compact cultivar 'Crispa' is lower and more spreading (18 inches tall, 3–4 feet wide) than the straight species and is the preferred selection for groundcover use. Branches root where they touch the ground, increasing spread.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; cutleaf stephanandra is a graceful, arching Japanese shrub in the rose family producing elegantly divided, deeply lobed, maple-like leaves with a refined, lacy texture that gives it its common name. In spring it produces clusters of small, white star-shaped flowers along the arching branches, attractive but secondary to the exceptional foliage. In autumn the leaves turn orange, red, and purple before dropping. The compact cultivar 'Crispa' is particularly valuable as a groundcover — growing only 18 inches tall while spreading 3–4 feet wide, with crimped, finely cut leaves that form a thick, weed-suppressing mat in shade. Branches that touch the ground root themselves (layering), helping the plant colonize large areas under trees where grass and other groundcovers fail. Among the finest shade-tolerant ornamental shrubs for the difficult zone between trees and planting beds, providing excellent weed suppression and year-round ground cover.

planting.note: Sow seed in early fall (45–65°F soil temperature) or early spring in well-prepared, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Kentucky bluegrass establishes slowly from seed (2–4 weeks to germinate, 6–8 weeks to usable turf) but forms a dense, self-repairing sod via underground rhizomes once established. It goes dormant and turns tan during drought or summer heat; resume growth in fall.

directions: Sow in fall or early spring in well-prepared, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to light shade; Kentucky bluegrass is the premier cool-season lawn grass of the northern United States — the standard against which other turf grasses are measured for fine texture, dense sod, deep-green color, and ability to repair itself through underground rhizomes (stolons). It produces a rich, blue-green, fine-textured carpet that performs best in cool (55–75°F) temperatures of spring and fall, going summer-dormant in heat and drought only to green up vigorously in autumn. The key advantage over other cool-season grasses is its rhizomatous spread — unlike bunch-forming tall fescue or ryegrass, Kentucky bluegrass fills in bare spots and repairs foot-traffic damage through vegetative spread. Establishing a Kentucky bluegrass lawn is slower from seed than from sod, but the resulting turf is superior in long-term density and durability. It is commonly blended with perennial ryegrass (for fast establishment) and fine fescues (for shade tolerance) in premium lawn seed mixes.
